legalnotice.para: This work is licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0 license.

revision.revremark: Initial release of opcgw user manual

Comprehensive update covering Epics 2-6: SQLite-backed persistence (WAL mode, connection pool), command queue with delivery confirmation, gRPC pagination, support for all metric types, gateway health metrics in OPC UA, stale-data status codes (Good/Uncertain/Bad), structured logging with microsecond timestamps + correlation IDs, configurable verbosity, performance-budget warnings, and remote-diagnostic operation taxonomy. Configuration chapter rewritten against actual TOML keys; new chapters on Logging & Observability and Operational Monitoring; new appendices for configuration reference, OPC UA address space, and operation names.

abstract.para: opcgw is a Rust-based gateway that bridges ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server with OPC UA clients for industrial automation and SCADA systems. It polls device metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals, persists them in a local SQLite database (WAL mode for concurrent reads), exposes them via OPC UA 1.04 with stale-data status codes, and accepts downlink commands from OPC UA clients with end-to-end delivery confirmation. This manual provides comprehensive guidance on installation, configuration, operation, observability, and troubleshooting.

section.title: What is opcgw?
section.para: opcgw (OPC Gateway) is a middleware application that bridges the gap between LoRaWAN sensor networks and industrial automation systems. It provides bidirectional integration between:

listitem.para: ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server — Manages LoRaWAN devices, gathers uplink metrics from distributed IoT sensors, and accepts downlink commands.
OPC UA Clients — SCADA systems, industrial controllers, and human-machine interfaces that consume metrics in real time and issue commands.

section.para: The gateway continuously polls device metrics from ChirpStack, persists them in a local SQLite database with WAL-mode concurrency, exposes the current values plus gateway health as OPC UA variables, and queues outbound commands for reliable delivery.

listitem.para: Real-time data collection — Polls metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals; supports counter, gauge, and absolute metric kinds plus the boolean, integer, float, and string OPC UA types.

Persistent storage — SQLite-backed current-value table (UPSERT semantics) plus an append-only metric_history table for historical analysis. WAL mode plus a per-task connection pool eliminates the prior in-memory Mutex bottleneck.

Reliable command delivery — A SQLite-backed FIFO command queue with parameter validation, ChirpStack downlink integration, and three-state delivery tracking (Pending → Sent → Confirmed / Failed) with configurable confirmation timeout.

OPC UA 1.04 server — Native endpoint built on async-opcua exposing every configured metric plus a synthetic Gateway folder containing health metrics (LastPollTimestamp, error_count, chirpstack_available).

Stale-data status codes — Each OPC UA read returns a status code derived from the metric's age: Good (within threshold), Uncertain (older but within 24 h), or Bad (older than 24 h). Operators see freshness without parsing timestamps.

Graceful degradation — Continues operating when ChirpStack is unreachable, when SQLite reports SQLITE_BUSY, when a poll cycle partially fails, or when individual devices return errors. Per-cycle error counts and outage signals are surfaced in logs and via OPC UA health metrics.

Structured observability — All log output is structured (key=value), uses microsecond UTC timestamps, and carries a per-OPC-UA-read request_id that propagates through storage and staleness checks. Per-operation budget warnings fire when opc_ua_read > 100 ms, storage_query > 10 ms, or batch_write > 500 ms.

Configurable verbosity — The global log level is controlled by the CLI -d count, the OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL environment variable, or the [logging].level TOML key (precedence in that order). Per-module log files capture at TRACE independently for post-mortem analysis.
gRPC pagination — Large device fleets are paginated through the ChirpStack List APIs with a configurable page size, preventing single-RPC overflows.
Cancellation-aware shutdown — The poller, OPC UA server, and pruner all share a CancellationToken; Ctrl-C (or SIGTERM) initiates a graceful drain with bounded latency.

section.title: System Architecture
section.para: opcgw follows a modular, message-passing architecture with a shared SQLite-backed storage layer. The poller, OPC UA server, and pruner each hold their own Arc<dyn StorageBackend> handle and coordinate via SQLite WAL mode rather than a Rust mutex.

listitem.para: Poller module (src/chirpstack.rs) — Authenticates to the ChirpStack gRPC API, polls metrics on a fixed cadence, classifies the metric kind (counter/gauge/absolute/unknown), validates values against the configured target type, and writes the cycle's metrics to SQLite as a single batched transaction. Tracks per-cycle error counts and emits a chirpstack_outage warning on the first connectivity failure of a cycle.

Storage layer (src/storage/) — Abstracted behind the StorageBackend trait with two implementations: SqliteBackend (production) and InMemoryBackend (test-only). The SQLite implementation uses a connection pool so each async task gets its own rusqlite::Connection, enabling concurrent readers and a single writer at the database level. Schema migrations run at startup. SQLite errors classified as DatabaseBusy or DatabaseLocked emit a storage_query warning labelled SQLITE_BUSY with a sibling sqlite_error_code field for differentiation.

OPC UA server (src/opc_ua.rs) — Builds the address space from the [[application]] sections in the configuration file, exposes each metric as a Variant with a staleness-derived StatusCode, and exposes the synthetic Gateway folder. Per-metric staleness transitions are tracked in a DashMap<(device_id, metric_name), StatusCode> for lock-free concurrent reads. Each read is wrapped in an info_span! so downstream storage_query and staleness_check logs inherit a request_id for correlation.

Configuration system (src/config.rs) — TOML-based (figment) with environment-variable overrides using the OPCGW_ prefix and __ as the nested-key separator. A two-phase init reads the [logging] block before tracing initialises so log-directory and verbosity decisions reach the file appender from the very first message.

Logging system (src/main.rs) — tracing subscriber with a console layer (stderr) plus five daily-rolling file appenders: a root opc_ua_gw.log and four per-module files (chirpstack.log, opc_ua.log, storage.log, config.log). All formatted with microsecond UTC timestamps via ChronoUtc.

Command queue (src/command_validation.rs + storage layer) — OPC UA write requests are validated against the [[application.device.command]] schemas, persisted to a SQLite-backed FIFO queue, dequeued by the poller, sent to ChirpStack via downlink, and tracked through delivery confirmation with a configurable timeout.

section.para: Data flow:
section.programlisting: ChirpStack gRPC API │ poll ▼ ChirpstackPoller ──── batch_write_metrics() ──▶ SqliteBackend (WAL) │ │ get_metric_value() ▼ OpcUa Server ──▶ OPC UA clients ▲ │ │ queue_command() │ writes ◀─────────────────┘

listitem.para: Any 64-bit processor (x86-64, ARM64). Two or more cores recommended for production so the poller, OPC UA server, and storage pruner can run on independent tokio worker threads.
Minimum 512 MB RAM; 2 GB recommended for deployments with 100+ devices. The SQLite WAL plus per-module log buffering dominate memory usage at steady state; per-metric overhead is negligible.

Minimum 100 MB free disk for the binary, configuration, and a small SQLite database. Allocate at least 1 GB for production deployments retaining 30 days of historical metrics with a 10 second polling interval. Log files (one root plus four per-module daily-rolling files) typically add 10-100 MB/day depending on the configured verbosity.
Outbound TCP to the ChirpStack gRPC endpoint (typically port 8080) and inbound TCP for OPC UA clients (default port 4855).
OS — Linux (Debian, Ubuntu, CentOS, Alpine), macOS, or Windows with Docker. The reference deployment uses Linux containers.
Rust toolchain — version 1.94.0 or later when building from source. The release binary has no Rust runtime requirement.
SQLite — bundled via rusqlite's bundled feature; no separate SQLite installation is needed.
ChirpStack — version 4.0 or later with the gRPC API enabled and an API token issued for the target tenant.
Firewall — outbound to ChirpStack (gRPC), inbound to OPC UA clients (port 4855 by default).

section.title: Installation via Docker (Recommended)
section.para: Docker installation is the quickest way to get opcgw running without compiling from source. The provided docker-compose.yml mounts a configuration directory, a data directory for the SQLite database and PKI certificates, and a log directory for the daily-rolling files.

step.para: Install Docker. Visit docker.com for instructions for your platform.
Edit config/config.toml to point at your ChirpStack server and define your applications, devices, and metrics (see Chapter 4).

step.screen: docker run -d \ --name opcgw \ -p 4855:4855 \ -v $(pwd)/config:/app/config:ro \ -v opcgw-data:/app/data \ -v opcgw-logs:/app/log \ guycorbaz/opcgw:latest

note.title: Container Volumes
note.para: The container exposes three mount points:

listitem.para: /app/config — configuration files, read-only at runtime.
/app/data — SQLite database files (opcgw.db, opcgw.db-wal) and the PKI directory.
/app/log — daily-rolling log files (set OPCGW_LOG_DIR=/app/log or [logging].dir = "/app/log").

section.title: Building from Source
section.para: Use a from-source build when you need platform-specific optimizations, debug symbols, or a custom Rust toolchain.

step.para: Verify the Rust toolchain (1.94.0 or later):
The compiled binary is at target/release/opcgw.
Run the gateway with an explicit configuration path:

note.title: Build Time
note.para: Release builds take roughly 5-10 minutes on a first compile (the protocol-buffer code is generated at build time via tonic-build). Subsequent incremental builds are measured in seconds.

section.title: Configuration Overview
section.para: opcgw is configured through a single TOML file (typically config/config.toml). Every value can be overridden by an environment variable. The location of the configuration file is resolved in this order:

listitem.para: The -c PATH CLI flag.
The CONFIG_PATH environment variable.
Default: the OPCGW_CONFIG_PATH/config.toml directory, where OPCGW_CONFIG_PATH defaults to config.

section.para: Environment variable overrides use the OPCGW_ prefix. Nested keys use __ (double underscore) as the separator. For example, [chirpstack].polling_frequency can be overridden by OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__POLLING_FREQUENCY=30. Two convenience aliases bypass the nesting convention:

listitem.para: OPCGW_LOG_DIR — quick override for [logging].dir, read directly in main.rs before tracing initialises.
OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL — quick override for [logging].level, read directly in main.rs.

section.title: [global] — Global Settings
section.para: The [global] section currently carries a single flag:
section.programlisting: [global] # Verbose internal debug output (currently a no-op operationally; # reserved for future debug-only behaviours that should not depend on # the tracing log level). debug = true
section.para: All log-related configuration lives under the dedicated [logging] section described next.

section.title: [logging] — Logging
section.para: The [logging] section configures the file log directory and the global verbosity level. Both keys are optional; sensible defaults apply when omitted.
section.programlisting:
[logging] # Directory where per-module daily-rolling log files are written. # Default: "./log" dir = "/var/log/opcgw"
# Global default log level: trace, debug, info, warn, error. # Default: "info" level = "info"

listitem.para: Directory containing the five daily-rolling log files (opc_ua_gw.log, chirpstack.log, opc_ua.log, storage.log, config.log). The directory is created with parents at startup if it does not already exist; if the configured directory is not writable, opcgw falls back to ./log and emits a stderr warning.

Default verbosity for the console (stderr) and the root opc_ua_gw.log. Per-module log files capture at TRACE independently of this value, so even at level = "error" you still get full per-module traces in chirpstack.log and friends.

section.para: Verbosity precedence (highest first):

listitem.para: CLI -d count (-d = debug, -dd = trace, -ddd = trace, etc.).
OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL environment variable.
OPCGW_LOGGING__LEVEL figment-style override.
[logging].level in the TOML file.
Hard-coded default: info.

section.para: Level semantics:

table.title: Log levels and their use cases

row.entry: Deepest debugging — every operation, every span entry
Production troubleshooting — key decisions, timings, IDs
Normal operations — cycle starts/ends, state transitions
Anomalies and retries that succeeded — early warning
Unrecoverable conditions only — silent if healthy

section.para: See for the operations reference, diagnostic patterns, and the symptom cookbook.

section.title: [chirpstack] — ChirpStack Connection
section.programlisting:
[chirpstack] # ChirpStack server address (gRPC endpoint, http:// or https://) server_address = "http://chirpstack.example.com:8080"
# API token issued by ChirpStack. NEVER commit a real token to VCS; # use OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN at deploy time. api_token = "your_api_token_here"
# Tenant UUID tenant_id = "ffffffff-ffff-ffff-ffff-ffffffffffff"
# Polling frequency in seconds (default: 10) polling_frequency = 10
# Connection retries when ChirpStack is unreachable (default: 10) retry = 10
# Delay (seconds) between two retries (default: 10) delay = 10
# Page size for pagination in list APIs (default: 100, range 1-1000) list_page_size = 100

warning.title: API Token Security
warning.para: The api_token grants full ChirpStack access. Do not commit production tokens. Override at deploy time:
The token is never written to log output (a security regression test in the suite asserts this).

section.title: [opcua] — OPC UA Server
section.programlisting:
[opcua] # Application identity (mandatory) application_name = "Chirpstack OPC UA Gateway" application_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway" product_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway"
# Diagnostics output from the OPC UA stack itself diagnostics_enabled = true
# Optional: TCP hello timeout (default 5 s) # hello_timeout = 10
# Optional: bind address (default: first interface IP) # host_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
# Optional: TCP port (default: 4840 for OPC UA, this project ships 4855) # host_port = 4855
# PKI configuration (relative paths are resolved under pki_dir) create_sample_keypair = true certificate_path = "own/cert.der" private_key_path = "private/private.pem" trust_client_cert = true check_cert_time = true pki_dir = "./pki"
# User authentication (single-user only at this time) user_name = "user1" user_password = "user1"
# Staleness threshold in seconds (Story 5-2) # Metrics older than this return Uncertain in OPC UA; # older than 24 h return Bad. Default: 120 (2 x default polling_frequency). # Range: 1-86400. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__STALE_THRESHOLD_SECONDS stale_threshold_seconds = 120

listitem.para: Drives the OPC UA StatusCode returned with each metric read. See .
When true, opcgw generates a self-signed certificate at first start. Set to false in production once you have provided real certificates under pki_dir.

warning.title: OPC UA Security
warning.para: The current build supports a single user/password and a basic PKI surface. Anonymous and certificate-only flows are not yet exposed. Plan production deployments behind a network boundary until Epic 7 (security hardening) lands.

section.title: [command_delivery] — Command Queue
section.programlisting:
[command_delivery] # Polling interval for ChirpStack delivery confirmations (default 5 s) status_poll_interval_secs = 5
# Maximum time to wait for ChirpStack to confirm a Sent command (default 60 s). # Commands still pending after this become Failed. confirmation_timeout_secs = 60
# Frequency of the timeout-detection scan (default 10 s) timeout_check_interval_secs = 10
# Retention period for completed commands (default 7 days). # Confirmed/Failed commands are purged after this window. history_retention_days = 7

section.title: [[application]] — Devices and Metrics
section.para: The address space exposed via OPC UA mirrors the configured application → device → metric hierarchy. Each application is an array entry under [[application]] and may contain any number of devices and metrics.
section.programlisting:
[[application]] application_id = "ae2012c2-75a1-407d-98ab-1520fb511edf" application_name = "Arrosage"
[[application.device]] device_name = "Niveau_citerne" device_id = "a840418371886840" # DevEUI
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Niveau_cit" # OPC UA browse name chirpstack_metric_name = "Water_deep_cm" # ChirpStack metric key metric_type = "Float" # Bool | Int | Float | String metric_unit = "cm" # optional, OPC UA EU info
[[application.device]] device_name = "Vanne01" device_id = "524d1e0a02243201"
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Status_v01" chirpstack_metric_name = "valveStatusCode" metric_type = "Int"
[[application.device.command]] command_name = "Open_close_valve01" command_id = 1 command_confirmed = true # request ChirpStack delivery confirmation command_port = 10 # downlink fPort

listitem.para: application_id must match a real ChirpStack application UUID — opcgw uses it to scope the List calls.
device_id must match the DevEUI of a real device.

[[application.device.read_metric]] declares uplink metrics; metric_type drives the OPC UA Variant type (Bool → Variant::Boolean, Int → Variant::Int32, Float → Variant::Double, String → Variant::String).

[[application.device.command]] declares a downlink command surface. OPC UA writes against the corresponding node enqueue a downlink with the configured command_id and command_port; when command_confirmed = true the queue tracks delivery state and times out per the [command_delivery] settings.

note.title: Constraints
note.para: Each TOML must contain at least one application, each application at least one device, each device at least one read metric (commands are optional). Metric names must be unique within a device.

section.title: Environment Variable Overrides
section.para: Every TOML key can be overridden via environment variables. The convention:

listitem.para: Standard nested-key override. Examples:
Convenience aliases read directly during the tracing-bootstrap phase, before the full TOML is loaded.
Overrides the path to config.toml when -c is not passed on the command line.

note.title: Why two aliases for log dir/level?
note.para: The [logging] block needs to be readable before the full configuration is parsed (otherwise the file appender cannot start). The convenience aliases are read by a standalone bootstrap step; the standard OPCGW_LOGGING__* overrides are picked up later when the full AppConfig loads. Both forms are supported; the alias wins if both are set.

listitem.para: Override the configuration file path. Wins over CONFIG_PATH and the default.
Increase verbosity. -d ⇒ debug; -dd or more ⇒ trace. Wins over every other verbosity source.
Print the help text and exit.
Print the version and exit.

section.title: Startup Verification
section.para: A successful start emits a sequence of structured log lines with microsecond UTC timestamps. Look for these checkpoints:

listitem.para: Tracing initialised (logging_init).
Configuration loaded (operation = "config_loaded" at info).
SQLite connection pool ready and migrations applied.
ChirpStack gRPC channel connected (operation = "chirpstack_connect", success = true).
OPC UA server bound to its endpoint and address space built.
First poll cycle completes (operation = "poll_cycle_start" followed by per-device device_polled debug lines).

example.title: Expected Startup Log Output
example.programlisting: 2026-04-27T12:34:56.123456Z INFO opcgw: tracing initialised level=info dir=./log 2026-04-27T12:34:56.234567Z INFO opcgw::config: operation="config_loaded" applications=4 devices=12 metrics=42 2026-04-27T12:34:56.300100Z INFO opcgw::storage::sqlite: schema_version=5 migrations_applied=0 2026-04-27T12:34:56.501020Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="chirpstack_connect" attempt=1 endpoint=http://... timeout_secs=0 2026-04-27T12:34:56.612030Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="chirpstack_connect" attempt=1 latency_ms=110 success=true 2026-04-27T12:34:56.700400Z INFO opcgw::opc_ua: OPC UA server started endpoint=opc.tcp://0.0.0.0:4855 2026-04-27T12:34:57.001234Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="poll_cycle_start" device_count=12

section.title: Graceful Shutdown
section.para: opcgw treats Ctrl-C and SIGTERM equivalently: a single CancellationToken is shared across the poller, OPC UA server, and pruner; on signal it cancels all three and the main task .awaits their drain.

listitem.para: Inbound OPC UA connections are refused; existing sessions are allowed to close in their own time.
The active poll cycle finishes its in-flight batch transaction (txn_commit) so no metrics are lost.
SQLite connections are returned to the pool and dropped; the WAL is checkpointed.
tracing's non-blocking appenders flush their buffers via their guard objects (held until main() returns).

warning.title: Force Shutdown
warning.para: Avoid SIGKILL (kill -9). The SQLite database is journaled and will recover, but in-flight downlink commands marked Sent may need manual inspection; log lines buffered in the non-blocking appender at the moment of the kill are lost. Always prefer SIGTERM.

section.title: What to Monitor
section.para: The gateway exposes its own health both in the OPC UA address space and through structured log lines. For a typical production deployment, the two-source approach is:

listitem.para: Live health — read the Gateway folder via OPC UA from your SCADA front-end. See .
Diagnostics — tail the per-module log files or aggregate them through your log shipper. See .

section.title: The Gateway Folder in OPC UA
section.para: Under the configured root, opcgw publishes a synthetic Gateway folder containing three health variables:

listitem.para: Time of the last successful poll cycle. The value is NULL (OPC UA Variant::Empty) until the first cycle completes; reading it before that emits a warn log line with warning="no_data_yet".

Cumulative per-cycle error count carried in the persisted gateway status row. Saturates at i32::MAX; a saturation read emits a warning. Spikes between consecutive cycles (delta >= 5) emit operation = "error_spike".
true when the most recent poll cycle succeeded for at least one device, false otherwise. The first per-cycle ChirpStack failure also emits operation = "chirpstack_outage".

section.para: All three reads are subject to the same staleness logic as regular metrics; under normal operation they return Good.

section.title: Stale-Data Status Codes
section.para: Every metric read returns a value plus an OPC UA StatusCode derived from the metric's age relative to [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds:

row.entry: Last-known value still served, but stale
Decisively stale; treat the value as suspect

section.para: Transitions between status codes emit operation = "staleness_transition": in-flight transitions at info, the cold-start first observation at debug (so restarts of a large stale fleet do not flood the alert pipeline). Metrics whose age sits within ±5 s of the threshold also emit operation = "staleness_boundary" at debug for diagnosability.

listitem.para: Time to complete a full metric poll cycle. Should remain below polling_frequency. Watch the per-cycle duration in the operation = "poll_cycle_complete" log line and the budget warning if operation = "batch_write" exceeds 500 ms.
Devices producing successful reads in the most recent cycle. The device_polled success=true count tells you the live fleet; a sudden drop is a signal to investigate ChirpStack or radio coverage.
SQLite database file size growth. The pruner enforces the historical retention window — if the database is growing faster than expected, check the operation = "prune_metric_history" line.

Per-read budget is 100 ms. The gateway emits a warn with exceeded_budget = true on any read past that bound; the Story 6-1 microbench measured p95 around 11 µs at 1 kHz read rate, so budget exceedance is a strong signal of contention.
Per-query budget is 10 ms. Persistent SQLITE_BUSY warnings indicate write contention or pool sizing problems.

section.title: Command Queue Monitoring
section.para: Downlink commands traverse three states:

listitem.para: Pending — Enqueued by an OPC UA write; not yet sent to ChirpStack.
Sent — ChirpStack accepted the downlink. If command_confirmed = true, the gateway polls for confirmation every status_poll_interval_secs.
Confirmed / Failed — Terminal states. A Sent command that does not transition within confirmation_timeout_secs becomes Failed.

section.para: Use the SQLite database to inspect queue state directly when needed:
section.screen: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db \ "SELECT command_id, status, created_at, sent_at, confirmed_at, error_message FROM commands ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;"

section.title: Architecture
section.para: opcgw uses the tracing crate end-to-end. Every log line is structured (key=value fields plus a free-text message), carries a microsecond UTC timestamp via ChronoUtc, and may carry a request_id if it was emitted inside an OPC UA read span.
Five appenders are wired by default:
All file appenders are daily-rolling. The level filter on per-module files is not configurable — operators always have the full per-module trace available even when the console is at error.

section.title: Request Correlation
section.para: Each OPC UA read is wrapped in an info_span! with a freshly generated request_id (UUID v4). All log lines emitted while that span is active inherit the field — including storage_query records from the SQLite layer and staleness_check records inside the OPC UA callback. To trace one client read end-to-end at debug:
The expected order is opc_ua_read entry → storage_query → staleness_check → opc_ua_read exit.

section.title: Performance Budgets
section.para: Three hot paths emit a structured warn with exceeded_budget = true when their wall-clock latency crosses a fixed threshold. The thresholds are constants in src/utils.rs:
Below budget the same call sites stay at debug, so steady-state operation does not flood the warn channel.

section.title: Operation Names — At a Glance
section.para: Every load-bearing log line carries an operation = "<name>" field. The full reference (including expected fields, level, and remediation tips) lives in docs/logging.md; the table below is a quick lookup of the most common names.

row.entry: poller, gRPC channel and probe paths
OPC UA server, every metric read
OPC UA server, every metric read
OPC UA server, on status code change
OPC UA server, ±5 s of threshold
OPC UA server, Gateway folder reads
OPC UA server, first uninitialised read

section.para: Future operations reserved for Story 4-4 (auto-recovery from ChirpStack outages): recovery_attempt, recovery_complete, recovery_failed.

section.title: Symptom Cookbook
section.para: Each subsection below pairs a symptom an operator can observe with the structured log lines that diagnose it and the corrective action.

section.title: Cannot connect to ChirpStack
section.para: Log signature: operation="chirpstack_connect" with success=false, repeated; or a single operation="chirpstack_outage" on the first per-cycle failure.

listitem.para: Confirm the configured server_address resolves and the gRPC port is reachable: nc -vz chirpstack.example.com 8080.

Validate the API token. A 16/Unauthenticated tonic code would surface as a generic OpcGwError::ChirpStack(...); cross-check by hitting ChirpStack directly with grpcurl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ chirpstack:8080 list.

Inspect last_successful_poll in the chirpstack_outage warn line: a long gap indicates a sustained outage. Story 4-4 will introduce automatic reconnection; until then, the poller continues attempting on its normal cadence.

section.title: Sudden burst of errors
section.para: Log signature: operation="error_spike" with delta >= 5.

listitem.para: Cross-reference the surrounding device_poll warns to identify the affected devices.
If a single fleet-wide gateway, check ChirpStack itself. If a localised group, check the radio path (rssi, snr in ChirpStack UI).
The spike is informational only — it does not gate further polling.

section.title: SQLite busy / locked warnings
section.para: Log signature: operation="storage_query" error="SQLITE_BUSY" sqlite_error_code=DatabaseBusy (or DatabaseLocked).

listitem.para: The sqlite_error_code field distinguishes DatabaseBusy (waiting on a lock) from DatabaseLocked (cross-connection write contention).

WAL mode and the per-task connection pool make rare SQLITE_BUSY tolerable. Sustained busy warnings under steady load suggest pool undersizing or a long-running write — verify the operation = "batch_write" latencies.
Run integrity check during a maintenance window: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;".

section.title: Performance budgets exceeded
section.para: Log signature: any of opc_ua_read, storage_query, or batch_write with exceeded_budget=true.

listitem.para: Capture the request_id on an opc_ua_read warn and grep the file logs for the full read-path trace.
If the bottleneck is storage_query, check query_type and the SQLITE_BUSY rate at the same time.
If batch_write is slow, the cumulative per-cycle metric count (metrics_count) plus disk I/O contention are the usual culprits.

section.title: OPC UA client sees NULL LastPollTimestamp
section.para: Log signature: operation="health_metric_read" metric="LastPollTimestamp" warning="no_data_yet"; once-per-process operation="gateway_status_init".

listitem.para: This is normal during startup, before the first poll cycle has completed. The gateway is healthy.
If it persists past one polling interval, the first cycle is failing — see .
Verify the OPC UA server bound successfully — search the log for "OPC UA server started".
Confirm the port is listening: ss -tlnp sport = :4855 (or netstat -tlnp | grep 4855).
Confirm the firewall permits inbound connections to the configured port.
If PKI is enabled, confirm the client certificate is under pki_dir/trusted/. Rejected certificates land in pki_dir/rejected/; copy them across explicitly to trust them.

section.title: OPC UA clients see Uncertain or Bad status
section.para: Log signature: operation="staleness_transition" status_code=Uncertain (or Bad); possibly operation="staleness_boundary" for metrics flickering near the threshold.

listitem.para: Map the affected metric to its device and check that the device is producing uplinks (ChirpStack UI).
Verify polling_frequency against stale_threshold_seconds: the latter should be at least 2× the former so transient missed cycles do not flap the status.
For a fleet-wide Uncertain wave that resolves on its own, suspect a brief ChirpStack outage; cross-check chirpstack_outage in the same time window.

section.title: Database integrity errors
section.para: Log signature: OpcGwError::Storage with messages mentioning "disk image malformed".

listitem.para: Stop the gateway gracefully (SIGTERM) so the WAL is checkpointed.
Back up the database before any recovery action: cp -p data/opcgw.db data/opcgw.db.backup.
Run the SQLite integrity check: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;". If it returns ok, the issue may have been transient; restart the gateway.
If integrity is broken, the gateway will recreate the schema on next start — but historical metrics are lost. Plan backups accordingly.

section.title: Database Maintenance
section.para: SQLite is largely self-maintaining under WAL mode. Two operations are worth scheduling:

step.para: Trigger SQLite's auto-tuner without stopping the gateway:
The pragma analyses indexes that have changed significantly and rebuilds plan statistics — runs in milliseconds on the production-sized database.

section.title: Historical Metric Pruning
section.para: The metric_history table is append-only and grows linearly with poll volume. opcgw runs a background pruner that deletes rows older than the configured retention window. Pruning is logged as operation = "prune_metric_history"; the line carries the cutoff timestamp and the row count deleted.

listitem.para: The retention window is read from the same TOML used at startup; no environment override.
Pruning is throttled by an internal retry-back-off so transient SQLite contention does not delay the next poll cycle.
To disable pruning, set the retention to a value larger than your operational horizon (the pruner has no "off" setting; setting retention to a long window achieves the same effect).

section.title: Command History Retention
section.para: Completed commands (Confirmed or Failed) are retained for [command_delivery].history_retention_days. The pruner runs alongside the metric pruner; both share the same retry and back-off logic.

section.title: Log Rotation
section.para: All five log appenders are daily-rolling: at UTC midnight a new file is created with the date suffix. opcgw does not delete old log files automatically; manage retention with your platform's standard tooling (logrotate, journald, Docker's logging driver, or a cron job under opcgw.cron.d).

appendix.title: Configuration Reference
appendix.para: Quick-lookup table for every TOML key. See for narrative descriptions and examples.

appendix.title: OPC UA Address Space
appendix.programlisting: Objects/ └── opcgw/ ├── Gateway/ │ ├── LastPollTimestamp (String, RFC 3339 or NULL) │ ├── error_count (Int32) │ └── chirpstack_available (Boolean) └── <application_name>/ (one folder per [[application]]) └── <device_name>/ (one folder per [[application.device]]) ├── <metric_name> (Variant per [[..read_metric]].metric_type) └── <command_name> (writable, per [[..command]])
appendix.para: Every read returns a StatusCode. For uplink metrics it is derived from the metric's age (Good / Uncertain / Bad). For Gateway-folder reads it is always Good, except that LastPollTimestamp may be NULL (still reported with Good status — the NULL value itself is the signal).

appendix.title: Operation Names
appendix.para: The complete reference of operation = ... values, expected fields, level, and remediation tips lives in docs/logging.md. A condensed table appears in . When adding a new operation, register it in docs/logging.md first so the log-analysis side has a contract to honour.
Reserved operation names (planned for future stories, not yet emitted):

listitem.para: recovery_attempt, recovery_complete, recovery_failed — Story 4-4 auto-recovery from ChirpStack outages.
Open-source LoRaWAN Network Server that manages devices and collects metrics.
Sharded concurrent hash map used by opcgw's OPC UA server to track per-metric staleness state without serialising reads on a single mutex.
Globally unique 64-bit identifier for a LoRaWAN end-device, used as the device_id in opcgw configuration.
Synthetic OPC UA folder under the configured root containing the gateway's own health metrics (LastPollTimestamp, error_count, chirpstack_available).
Remote-procedure-call framework built on HTTP/2 used by ChirpStack's API.
Long-Range Wide-Area Network protocol for low-power IoT devices.
OPC Unified Architecture, a machine-to-machine communication standard for industrial automation.
Per-OPC-UA-read UUID v4 emitted as a tracing-span field; all log lines emitted while the span is active inherit it, enabling end-to-end correlation by simple grep.
Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition system for monitoring and controlling industrial processes.
Write-Ahead Logging journaling mode that allows multiple readers to operate concurrently with a single writer. opcgw enables WAL at startup; it is the foundation of the lock-free read path.
OPC UA per-read status indicator. opcgw maps metric freshness to Good, Uncertain, or Bad.
The Rust crate providing structured, span-aware logging. opcgw emits all log output through it.
Write-Ahead Log — see SQLite WAL mode.
